title: Apply: specific heat capacity
description: Apply your understanding of specific heat capacity in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A sculpture in a park features two globes, one made of gold and one made of bronze. Both globes have the same approximate mass. Their specific heat capacities are as follows: 

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-: 
Bronze | $0.435$
Gold | $0.129$

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

The globes are warmed by the sun on a hot summer morning. They both absorb the same amount of thermal energy. 

**Complete the statements.**

* By noon, the [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] globe will reach the highest temperature.

* This occurs because this globe has a [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] specific heat capacity.

* As a result, it requires [[‚ėÉ dropdown 3]] thermal energy to increase its temperature compared to the other globe.
hints: The specific heat capacity of a substance is the amount of thermal energy required to raise $1$ gram of that substance by $1\degree\text{C}$.

If we compare an equal mass of two substances, the substance with the *lower* heat capacity will require less energy to change its temperature by $1\degree\text{C}$ than the substance with the *higher* heat capacity.

For example, gold has a specific heat capacity of $0.129 \text { J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of gold must absorb $0.129$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Bronze has a higher specific heat capacity: $0.435 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of bronze must absorb $0.435$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Compared to gold, that‚Äôs almost three times as much energy!
So, because both globes have the same mass, if they absorb the same amount of thermal energy, the gold globe will experience a *greater* change in temperature.

So, the complete statements are:

* By noon, the gold globe will reach the highest temperature.

* This occurs because this globe has a lower specific heat capacity.

* As a result, it requires less thermal energy to increase its temperature compared to the other globe.

options.alt: A bronze statue of Nicolaus Copernicus holding a golden globe.
options.caption: *A statue of Nicolaus Copernicus*

assessment_items.question_content:
A gardener is designing a tile walkway through her garden. She plans to walk barefoot in her garden on hot summer days, so she wants to use a tile material that will remain *as cool as possible* in the sun.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

The gardener can choose from tiles made from the following materials. Assume that all tiles have the same approximate mass. 

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-: 
Brick | $0.92$
Glass | $0.53$
Slate | $0.76$
Wood | $2.31$

**Complete the statements.**

* Tiles made from [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] would remain the coolest on a summer day. 

* This occurs because this material has [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] specific heat capacity.

* Therefore, it takes [[‚ėÉ dropdown 3]] thermal energy to raise the temperature compared to the other tiles.
hints: The specific heat capacity of a substance is the amount of thermal energy required to raise $1$ gram of that substance by $1\degree\text{C}$.

If we compare an equal mass of two substances, the substance with the *lower* heat capacity will require less energy to change its temperature by $1\degree\text{C}$ than the substance with the *higher* heat capacity.

For example, let‚Äôs compare the given substances with the lowest and the highest specific heats. Glass has a specific heat capacity of $0.53 \text { J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of glass must absorb $0.53$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Wood has a higher specific heat capacity: $2.31 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of wood must absorb $2.31$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Compared to glass, that‚Äôs over four times as much energy!

So, a wooden tile requires *more* energy to change temperature compared to the other tiles. As a result, a wooden tile will experience a *smaller* change in temperature and remain cooler than the other tiles.

So, the complete statements are:

* Tiles made from wood would remain the coolest on a summer day. 

* This occurs because this material has higher specific heat capacity.

* Therefore, it takes more thermal energy to raise the temperature compared to the other tiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of engineering students is constructing a video game console. They need to equip the console with a **heat sink**‚ÄĒa component that absorbs heat from the CPU *without a significant change in temperature*. 

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

They have three choices of metal for the heat sink: aluminum, gold foil, and tin. Assume the heat sinks would all have the same approximate mass.

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-: 
Aluminum | $0.897$ 
Gold | $0.129$
Tin | $0.227$

**Complete the statements.**

* The best metal to use for the heat sink is [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]].

* This occurs because this metal has [[‚ėÉ dropdown 4]] specific heat capacity.

* Therefore, it takes [[‚ėÉ dropdown 5]] thermal energy to change its temperature compared to the other metals.
hints: The specific heat capacity of a substance is the amount of thermal energy required to raise $1$ gram of that substance by $1\degree\text{C}$.

If we compare an equal mass of two substances, the substance with the *lower* heat capacity will require less energy to change its temperature by $1\degree\text{C}$ than the substance with the *higher* heat capacity.

For example, let's compare the substances with the lowest and the highest specific heats. Gold has a specific heat capacity of $0.129 \text { J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of gold must absorb $0.129$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Aluminum has a higher specific heat capacity: $0.897 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This means that $1$ gram of aluminum must absorb $0.897$ joules of energy to warm up by $1\degree\text{C.}$ Compared to gold, that‚Äôs almost seven times as much energy!

So, an aluminum heat sink requires *more* energy to change temperature compared to the other metals. As a result, an aluminum heat sink will experience a *smaller* change in temperature than the other metals.

So, the complete statements are:

* The best metal to use for the heat sink is aluminum.

* This occurs because this metal has the highest specific heat capacity.

* Therefore, it takes more thermal energy to change its temperature compared to the other metals.

options.alt: A photograph of a hint sink inside a computer.
options.caption: *A typical heat sink*

assessment_items.question_content:
Some recipes require a preheated pan before adding the first ingredients. A larger, more massive pan takes longer to heat up than a smaller pan made of the same material.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

**What is the primary reason the larger pan takes longer to preheat?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]

options.alt: A photograph of different-sized copper pans, each nestling into the other.
options.caption: *A set of copper pans*

choices.content: It has the same specific heat capacity, but contains more atoms. More thermal energy is required to raise their average kinetic energy.
It has a greater mass, so it has a higher specific heat capacity. More thermal energy is required to reach the desired temperature.
It has a larger surface area, so it has a lower specific heat capacity. More heat must be transferred to the pan to raise its temperature.

assessment_items.question_content:
You may have heard the old saying, ‚ÄúA watched pot never boils.‚ÄĚ While that may not be actually true, it does take longer to heat up a large pot of water than a small pot of water.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

**What is the primary reason the larger pot takes longer to boil?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]

options.alt: A photograph of a large pot of boiling water.
options.caption: *Ready for the pasta!*

choices.content: The water in both pots has the same specific heat capacity, but there are more water molecules in the larger pot. More thermal energy is required to raise their average kinetic energy.
The water in the large pot has a greater mass, so it has a higher specific heat capacity. More energy is required to reach the boiling point.
The water in the large pot has a larger surface area, so it has a lower specific heat capacity. More heat must be transferred to the water to raise its temperature.

assessment_items.question_content:
Many recipes call for melting butter in a pan. If a large piece of butter is placed in a pan alongside a smaller piece of butter, the smaller piece melts first.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

**Why does the smaller piece of butter melt more quickly?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]

options.alt: A photograph of a pan being held over a stove. Several differently-sized pieces of butter are melting.
options.caption: *Melting butter in a pan*

choices.content: Both pieces of butter have the same specific heat capacity, but there are fewer molecules in the smaller piece. Less thermal energy is required to raise their average kinetic energy.
It has a lower mass, so it has a lower specific heat capacity. Less energy is required to reach the desired temperature.
It has a smaller surface area, so it has a higher specific heat capacity. Less heat must be transferred to the butter to raise its temperature.

assessment_items.question_content:
One way of joining together different pieces of metal is to use *solder*. Solder is a soft metal alloy with a low melting point. An electric heater or blowtorch is used to melt the solder. As it cools it re-solidifies, bonding the metals into one solid piece. 

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

A certain type of solder has a specific heat capacity of $0.221  \text{ J/g¬įC}$ and a melting point of $237.0\degree\text{C}$. 

A welder uses a propane torch to heat $3.23$ grams of this solder from room temperature $(22.2\degree\text{C})$ to its melting point. 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 3]]

**How much thermal energy did the solder absorb?**  
*Report your answer to the nearest whole number.*

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 1]] joules

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]
hints: This question asks how much thermal energy is needed to raise the temperature of a sample of solder. We are given the mass, specific heat, and initial and final temperatures of the solder. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT.$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the solder was $22.2\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is its melting point, $237.0\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 237.0\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  22.2\text{¬įC}$

$‚ąÜT = 214.8\text{¬įC}$

Now let‚Äôs calculate $q.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the specific heat equation:

$q = mc‚ąÜT$ 

$q = 3.23 \text{ g} \times 0.221 \text{ J/g¬įC} \times 214.8\text{¬įC}$

$q = 153.3307 \text{ J}$

Rounding the answer correctly to three significant figures gives us $153$ joules.
So, the solder absorbed $153$ joules of thermal energy.

options.explanation: ‚Äú[Propane torch soldering copper pipe](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Propane_torch_soldering_copper_pipe.jpg)‚ÄĚ by neffk, [CC BY-SA 2.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0/)

options.alt: A photograph of two copper pipes being soldered together using a piece of solder and a propane blow-torch.
options.caption: *Two copper pipes being soldered together*

assessment_items.question_content:
NASA‚Äôs Mars Exploration Rovers need sophisticated systems to maintain a stable temperature range. One of those systems uses liquid Freon-12 to absorb heat from the rover‚Äôs batteries. This liquid must be kept within a strict temperature range at all times.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

Liquid Freon-12 has a specific heat capacity of $0.95  \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ Over the course of an hour, $10.3$ grams of the liquid warms from $-7.1\degree\text{C}$ to $0.2\degree\text{C}.$ 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 3]]

**How much thermal energy did the liquid absorb?**  
*Report your answer to the nearest whole number.*

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 1]] joules

[[‚ėÉ explanation 4]]
hints: This question asks how much thermal energy is needed to raise the temperature of a sample of Freon-12. We are given the mass, specific heat, and initial and final temperatures of Freon-12. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT.$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the liquid was $-7.1\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is $0.2\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature. Don't forget the negative sign on $-7.1\text{¬įC!}$

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 0.2\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  (-7.1\text{¬įC})$

$‚ąÜT = 7.3\text{¬įC}$

Now let‚Äôs calculate $q.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the specific heat equation:

$q = mc‚ąÜT$ 

$q = 10.3 \text{ g} \times 0.95 \text{ J/g¬įC} \times 7.3\text{¬įC}$

$q = 71.4305 \text{ J}$

Rounding the answer correctly to two significant figures gives us $71$ joules.
So, the Freon-12 liquid absorbed $71$ joules of thermal energy.

options.explanation: ‚Äú[Artist Concept of Rover on Mars](https://images.nasa.gov/details/PIA04413)‚ÄĚ by NASA/JPL/Cornell University, [NASA Public Domain](https://www.nasa.gov/multimedia/guidelines/index.html)

options.alt: An artists conception of the Mars Exploration Rover on the surface of Mars.
options.caption: *NASA artist's conception of the Martian rover*

assessment_items.question_content:
The element gallium is a soft, silver-colored metal with a melting point of $29.8\degree\text{C}$. Since this is lower than human body temperature, gallium can melt in a person's hand!

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

The specific heat capacity of gallium is $0.374  \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ A piece of gallium with a mass of $11.3$ grams is removed from refrigerated storage at $4.5\degree\text{C}$ and heated to its melting point. 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 3]]

**How much thermal energy did the gallium absorb?**  
*Report your answer to the nearest whole number.*

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 1]] joules
hints: This question asks how much thermal energy is needed to raise the temperature of a sample of gallium. We are given the mass, specific heat, and initial and final temperatures of the sample. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT.$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the gallium was $4.5\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is its melting point, $29.8\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 29.8\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  4.5\text{¬įC}$

$‚ąÜT = 25.3\text{¬įC}$

Now let‚Äôs calculate $q.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the specific heat equation:

$q = mc‚ąÜT$ 

$q = 11.3 \text{ g} \times 0.374 \text{ J/g¬įC} \times 25.3\text{¬įC}$

$q = 106.923 \text{ J}$

Rounding the answer correctly to three significant figures gives us $107$ joules.
So, the gallium absorbed $107$ joules of thermal energy.

options.alt: A photograph of a man holding a piece of gallium that's melting in his hand.
options.caption: *Gallium melts below human body temperature*

assessment_items.question_content:
A lab group is given $25.0$ grams of an unknown metal. The students are instructed to identify the metal by determining its specific heat capacity. Their unknown must be one of the metals from the following table:

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-:  
Aluminum | $0.897$
Magnesium | $1.024$
Silver | $0.235$
Zinc | $0.388$

Using a standard calorimetry set-up and three separate trials, the students report that their sample released $‚Äď1760$ joules of thermal energy when it cooled from $100.0\text{¬įC}$ to $21.4\text{¬įC.}$ 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

**What is the likely identity of the metal?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: This question asks us to calculate the specific heat of an unknown metal. We are given the mass of the metal, the heat lost by the metal during cooling, and the initial and final temperatures of the metal. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT$.

The variable we‚Äôre looking for is $c,$ the specific heat capacity of the unknown metal. Let‚Äôs rearrange the equation. 

To solve for $c,$ we must divide both sides of the equation by $m‚ąÜT$:

$\dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT} = \dfrac{\bcancel{m}c\bcancel{‚ąÜT}}{\bcancel{m‚ąÜT}}$

Canceling our variables and rearranging for $c,$ we are left with:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the metal was $100.0\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is $21.4\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 21.4\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  100.0\text{¬įC}$

$‚ąÜT = -78.6\text{¬įC}$

The negative sign means the temperature decreased, so heat was *lost* by the metal. That‚Äôs also why $q$ is negative $1760$ joules.

Now let‚Äôs calculate $c.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the rearranged equation:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

$c = \dfrac{-1760\text{ J}}{25.0\text{ g} \times -78.6\text{¬įC}}$

$c = 0.8956743 \text{ J/g¬įC}$

Rounding the answer correctly to three significant figures gives us a specific heat of $0.896 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This is most similar to the specific heat capacity of aluminum.
So, the likely identity of the metal is aluminum.

assessment_items.question_content:
A lab group is given $42.0$ grams of an unknown metal. The students are instructed to identify the metal by determining its specific heat capacity. Their unknown must be one of the metals from the following table:

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-:  
Cobalt | $0.421$
Iron | $0.449$
Lead | $0.127$
Zinc | $0.388$

Using a standard calorimetry set-up and three separate trials, the students report that their sample released $‚Äď418$ joules of thermal energy when it cooled from $99.6\text{¬įC}$ to $20.8\text{¬įC.}$ 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

**What is the likely identity of the metal?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: This question asks us to calculate the specific heat of an unknown metal. We are given the mass of the metal, the heat lost by the metal during cooling, and the initial and final temperatures of the metal. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT$.

The variable we‚Äôre looking for is $c,$ the specific heat capacity of the unknown metal. Let‚Äôs rearrange the equation. 

To solve for $c,$ we must divide both sides of the equation by $m‚ąÜT$:

$\dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT} = \dfrac{\bcancel{m}c\bcancel{‚ąÜT}}{\bcancel{m‚ąÜT}}$

Canceling our variables and rearranging for $c,$ we are left with:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the metal was $99.6\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is $20.8\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 20.8\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  99.6\text{¬įC}$

$‚ąÜT = -78.8\text{¬įC}$

The negative sign means the temperature decreased, so heat was *lost* by the metal. That‚Äôs also why $q$ is negative $418$ joules.

Now let‚Äôs calculate $c.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the rearranged equation:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

$c = \dfrac{-418\text{ J}}{42.0\text{ g} \times -78.8\text{¬įC}}$

$c = 0.126299 \text{ J/g¬įC}$

Rounding the answer correctly to three significant figures gives us a specific heat of $0.126 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This is most similar to the specific heat capacity of lead.
So, the likely identity of the metal is lead.

assessment_items.question_content:
A lab group is given $12.5$ grams of unknown metal. The students are instructed to identify the metal by determining its specific heat capacity. Their unknown must be one of the metals from the following table:

Substance | Specific heat $\text{(J/g¬įC)}$ 
- | :-:  
Copper | $0.384$
Gold | $0.129$
Nickel | $0.445$
Silver | $0.235$

Using a standard calorimetry set-up and three separate trials, the students report that their sample released $‚Äď352$ joules of thermal energy when it cooled from $98.4\text{¬įC}$ to $24.7\text{¬įC.}$ 

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

**What is the likely identity of the metal?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: This question asks us to calculate the specific heat of an unknown metal. We are given the mass of the metal, the heat lost by the metal during cooling, and the initial and final temperatures of the metal. We are also given the specific heat equation, $q = mc‚ąÜT$.

The variable we‚Äôre looking for is $c,$ the specific heat capacity of the unknown metal. Let‚Äôs rearrange the equation. 

To solve for $c,$ we must divide both sides of the equation by $m‚ąÜT$:

$\dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT} = \dfrac{\bcancel{m}c\bcancel{‚ąÜT}}{\bcancel{m‚ąÜT}}$

Canceling our variables and rearranging for $c,$ we are left with:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

Before we can use the equation, we need to calculate $‚ąÜT$. We know the initial temperature of the metal was $98.4\text{¬įC,}$ and the final temperature is $24.7\text{¬įC.}$

To find the change in temperature, we subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$‚ąÜT = Tf \,‚Äď\, Ti$

$‚ąÜT = 24.7\text{¬įC}\,\ ‚Äď\,  98.4\text{¬įC}$

$‚ąÜT = -73.7\text{¬įC}$

The negative sign means the temperature decreased, so heat was *lost* by the metal. That‚Äôs also why $q$ is negative $352$ joules.

Now let‚Äôs calculate $c.$ We do that by plugging our knowns into the rearranged equation:

$c = \dfrac{q}{m‚ąÜT}$

$c = \dfrac{-352\text{ J}}{12.5\text{ g} \times -73.7\text{¬įC}}$

$c = 0.38209 \text{ J/g¬įC}$

Rounding the answer correctly to three significant figures gives us a specific heat of $0.382 \text{ J/g¬įC.}$ This is most similar to the specific heat capacity of copper.
So, the likely identity of the metal is copper.
